pattern: The set of positions a single marked cell can jump to in one fixed-size-window reversal forms a contiguous arithmetic range of one parity, so shortest-distance-to-every-cell reduces to a layered sweep over that range instead of a graph with an explicit edge list.
hints: Only reversals whose window currently contains the marked position can move it, and every other reversal is a wasted step, so think of this as breadth-first search over positions rather than over raw operations.

For a window with left edge l and length k that contains position i, the marked cell lands at l + (l + k - 1) - i; as l sweeps across every window that still contains i, the landing spot sweeps a contiguous set of positions, all sharing one parity, in steps of two.

Compute that reachable interval's low and high ends from i, k and n directly (clamping the window's left edge to stay in bounds), then in each breadth-first layer mark every still-unvisited, non-banned position of the matching parity inside that interval as reached one step later.

edge_inputs.description: starting position is not zero and one reversal of the whole array is the only move
an early banned position blocks all further reachability
window size 1 never moves the marked cell, so every other position is unreachable
p starts at position 0, distance to itself is 0
p sits completely surrounded by banned positions on both sides, trapping it at distance 0 only
no banned positions at all, every reachable position should get a finite distance
k equals n, the only operation reverses the entire array every time
n is 1, the single position is p itself with nothing else to reach

stress.description: random n up to 200 with a random start, random window size, and a modest random banned set excluding p
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 200))
    p = rng.randrange(0, n)
    k = rng.randrange(1, n + 1)
    max_banned = min(n - 1, 40)
    banned_count = rng.randrange(0, max_banned + 1) if n > 1 else 0
    candidates = [x for x in range(n) if x != p]
    rng.shuffle(candidates)
    banned = sorted(candidates[:banned_count])
    return [n, p, banned, k]
stress.note: n capped at 200 keeps the O(n^2)-ish oracle and BFS well under the time budget while still exercising many parities and window sizes

solutions.python:
class Solution:
    def minReverseOperations(self, n, p, banned, k):
        banned_set = set(banned)
        dist = [-1] * n
        dist[p] = 0
        unvisited = [set(), set()]
        for x in range(n):
            if x != p and x not in banned_set:
                unvisited[x % 2].add(x)
        frontier = [p]
        d = 0
        while frontier:
            next_frontier = []
            for i in frontier:
                lo = 2 * max(0, i - k + 1) + k - 1 - i
                hi = 2 * min(i, n - k) + k - 1 - i
                if lo > hi:
                    continue
                bucket = unvisited[lo % 2]
                reached = [x for x in bucket if lo <= x <= hi]
                for x in reached:
                    bucket.discard(x)
                    dist[x] = d + 1
                    next_frontier.append(x)
            frontier = next_frontier
            d += 1
        return dist

complexity.time: O(n^2) worst case for the layered sweep
